speaker: interviewer
text: --Sinason. And you were talking about a little door. Can you expand on that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was describing the arrangement of the trains in those days. As a matter of fact, they still have them in Europe today, where passengers sit in little compartments. The compartments have two benches. On each bench, there are three seats. So there are six people in the compartment, and there's a little door that shuts each compartment. And outside the door, there's a long hallway, corridor, that goes from one end of the railroad car to the other. And the restrooms are at the end. And those were the configurations of the trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after the Germans had let me go and we went back onto the train, this couple that had charge of me said, OK, we've passed the Germans. Now there will be about a 10-minute ride, and then we stop for the French control.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what I want you to do now is you crawl into the space under the bench. And we will sit on top of you, on the bench. And you will be behind our legs. And you'll be very still. Don't even breathe when they come in. They will come in. They will say something in French, and we will show them our passports. And then hopefully, they will go away. And there were some other people in the compartment too. They were all Jewish people, leaving Germany, fortunately. And I did that. And sure enough, after about 10 minutes or so, the train stopped again. And after a few minutes, the door to the compartment opened. I saw a uniform that looked very strange. Obviously it was a French customs inspector. And he asked for the papers. They showed him the papers, and the man saluted. After about two minutes, the train went on. And I was in France. The train arrived in Paris, about 6 o'clock in the morning. But there was a little problem. Because unbeknownst to me, my father had bought a second class ticket, and we were in first class with these people. And when the French ticket controller came along to verify the tickets, he said I had to pay an extra 50 francs. Well, I didn't have a dime with me, either German money or French money.

speaker: interviewee
text: So these people said, well-- 50 francs in those days was oh, the equivalent of about $25 perhaps-- said well, we'll pay it. But we'll have to go and get it back from your uncle, when he meets us at the station. And I said fine with me. I mean, you know. Nothing I could do about it. Who how can people that you were traveling with?

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't know them. They were total strangers, they were total strangers. But apparently, they had French citizenship, and they could travel freely between the two countries. I never saw them again after this adventure, and we had never seen them before. And I was purposely-- they never gave us their name or anything. But of course, they got paid for this. That was their job. They were smuggling children out of Germany, Jewish children. My father was supposed to have gotten in touch with my uncle through a friend who had a telephone, so that my uncle would meet us at the station, when we arrived in Paris. It was about 6 o'clock in the morning. But of course, my uncle wasn't there, because he had never been contacted.

speaker: interviewee
text: So these people who wanted their money said, well we'll go-- do you have his address? I said, yes. So we went, took a taxi, and we went to my uncle's house. And we knocked on his door, at like 6:30 in the morning, and everybody was sound asleep. And he came in his pajamas to the door. And he recognized me. And he said, what are you doing here? And then he saw the other people. And he said, oh, boy, another one.

speaker: interviewee
text: In other words, he was familiar with this. And he understood what had happened. And he paid the people. And they said thank you and goodbye, and that was the last that I saw of them. I never knew who they were or what.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was at my uncle's house. And my uncle was a little angry. He said, why didn't your father get in touch with me, et cetera. So I told him the story, that he only had 48 hours. And I said he tried to get in touch with you. And he was-- he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't get the message. The message came about four hours later. [LAUGHS] Anyway, so here I was. And then I was supposed to call my parents, to let them know that I was safe in Paris, which meant that my brother could go the same way. And I was supposed to do it the same day, which I did. And my brother came the same way, two days later. And my parents went-- that same day that my brother left, they left on another train to Hamburg and went on this ship and went to China. And this was in January, the end of January, 1939. And I did not hear from or see my parents again until 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your aunt and uncle's names again?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle's name was Richard. My father has six brothers and three sisters. And this uncle was Richard Sinason. And his wife's name was Greta Sinason.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had one daughter, my cousin. Her name was Renee. All three of them are deceased now, unfortunately. An important point for my later story is to note that my aunt Greta was not born Jewish. She converted when she married my uncle. So she had kept her German citizenship. She had a German passport, which was not stamped with the big J for Jew. It was a valid German passport. And this, again, as you will hear later on, is a very important point in my story.

speaker: interviewer
text: What papers did you have with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What papers did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Papers? I had a German passport, which was marked with a big J, which meant Jew. And every Jew in Germany, when he was male, had to take on the middle name of Israel. And every female had to take on the middle name of Sarah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my passport read Heinz Israel Sinason. And it had on the front of it a big red J, the letter J. That's all I had.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had a copy of this communication from the German consulate in Berlin, which said your quota number to immigrate to the United States is 22,800 and something. I don't remember the exact number. But of course, it was addressed to my parents. You and your family are, you know. And we were supposed to keep that and give that to the American consul in Paris or wherever we would wind up, so that we could be notified if and when our quota would come up, and we could make efforts to go to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you live with your aunt and uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived there at the end of January. My uncle was not too well off financially. He was working as a salesman. And he was making ends meet, but it was hard for him to really support two extra boys. We went to-- he enrolled us in a French school. I had completed elementary school in Germany, so I had to go to what the French call lycee, which is sort of high school to start with. But it goes a little further than our American high schools. And we had to learn French. Fortunately, I've always had a good ear. And I picked up French very quickly. It only took a few months, and I could really communicate fairly well. We stayed with my uncle until World War II broke out. And it broke out between Germany and France and England on September 3, 1939. Now my uncle, being from Germany and had applied to become a French citizen, but the waiting period in France is seven years. So he was not yet a French citizen, he was still German. And with that, he was now an enemy alien in France. And the French rounded up the enemy aliens. Because they couldn't tell which ones were good Germans and which ones were bad Germans. So they rounded up the Jews with the other Gentile Germans. And they put them into labor camps. And their job was to repair roads, repair bridges, dig trenches for the troops, and do things of that sort. They were not harmed in any way or threatened physically. But they had to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: With that of course, my aunt had no financial means to keep us. And she called an acquaintance, who was working for a Jewish social agency in Paris called the [FRENCH], which means medical assistance group to help refugee children. These people sent us to a children's home, boarding school in the suburbs of Paris. There were about 25 children there. It was a large country house. It was run by a German Jewish couple. And we were enrolled in the little village school there and continued our education.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we helped with the chores and kept that house running. And the social organization paid for our maintenance, as it did for all the kids that were there. There were a total of-- did I say 20 or 25?

speaker: interviewee
text: And all went well.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this, exactly, and how old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in September of 1939. I had just turned 14. Now as I said, this little children's home was run by a German Jewish couple. And about a couple of months after we got there, the gentleman that was running this home-- this Jewish man from Germany, his name was Mr Joachim-- was also rounded up by the French police as an enemy alien and sent away. And his wife tried to carry on for a while without him. But it was very difficult for her to run this thing alone. And eventually we were transferred to another similar home in another French-- in another Paris suburb called Orsay. And there was this little children's home, very similar to the one that we had been in. Except it was run by two Polish ladies, Jewish ladies who were from Poland. And as such, they were not enemy aliens, as far as the French were concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I recall only the first name of one of the ladies. Her name was Bella. And she had a long Polish last name. I can't remember it, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Orsay was not very far from the first home, which was located in a suburb called Palaiseau. So Do you want me to spell these French names or is that necessary? It's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: No? OK. Orsay and Palaiseau were about six kilometers apart, about four miles. So it was possible for us to continue in the same school, although we had a long walk. We walked about four miles, four times a day. Because we went home for lunch and back to school after lunch and back in the afternoon. And we did that-- [LAUGHS] for a good many months. It was nice in the summer, but not so nice in the winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the age group of the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: The age group of the children was about-- I think the oldest there was 16, and the youngest was about eight or nine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had-- this was a country house. And they had about four or five bedrooms. And we slept dormitory style, the beds were very close together. And five or six or seven kids to a room. And we managed, we managed. We were still being subsidized by this Jewish social agency in Paris. And we're now talking about the beginning of 1940, the spring of 1940, February, March, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you do during the day, with your time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about--

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to school from-- school started at 8:30 in the morning and didn't get out till 4:30 in the afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about when you weren't going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we weren't going to school, well, besides doing our school work, we helped to run the house. We had planted vegetable garden, to simply have food to eat. We helped with that. We kept up the grounds. As all children do, we played a little bit. We did whatever sports we could. We played among ourselves, a little soccer and did errands, helped the ladies go shopping, carried the bundles. And you know, did-- and bedtime was fairly early. Because we had to wake up very early in the morning to trudge our four miles to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there wasn't all that much free time. And there was homework to do. Did you know what was going on with the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew what was going on with the war, because we were subject to air raids. And many a night, the sirens would go off, and we had to go into the basement, into the cellar. And this was both when we were at Palaiseau and at Orsay. And we had rigged up some straw bunks, so that we could sleep in the cellar. And we would hear bombs go off. Because Paris was fortified in a strange way. They had built a ring of forts, fortifications, in a circle, around Paris. And we lived right sort of under one of these forts, which was up on a little hilltop. And in these forts, they had anti-aircraft guns. And the German planes used those gun emplacements as their favorite target. And as kids, in the morning-- they always came at night-- in the morning, we would go out. And we would find these pieces of shrapnel from the exploded bombs. And we'd collect those as souvenirs. None of the children was ever hurt or in any way-- there was damage to the house, windows broken and things of that sort. Doors that would hang askew. But nothing serious ever happened to us. We were not that close to the target. We were maybe half a mile away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we knew about the war. And of course, there were newspapers. And there was a radio. And we heard talk from our teachers and what have you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Patriotism ran very high. We made friends with the French kids in school. The teachers were very sympathetic to us. And what we had gone through in Germany and what have you, they helped us-- my teacher, at least-- my homeroom teacher helped in every way she could. She helped me with my French, and she helped me in every other way. I was a good student. I had-- by that time, I was fluent in French and kept up with the others. And I really wasn't any problem. Some of the other kids had more of a tough time with the French. But I-- I didn't. So I was lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get any correspondence from your parents, or did you know anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had no news at all. We tried through the Red Cross to find out what had happened to them. And the only news that we received was that there were 20,000 Jews in Shanghai. They lived in a ghetto. And they were not being harmed. If we could send food packages, we should, which was a little facetious, because we didn't have enough to eat ourselves. Food by that time was rationed in France. And of course, we couldn't send any packages to them. And the Red Cross sent some forms. And they said, well, you can fill out this form. And on the bottom, there are three lines for a personal message. And maybe we can get this through, and maybe not. We can't promise, but you can try. So we did that, but it didn't help.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in these places? I mean, you were in the two different places--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we are now sort of at the end of spring of 1940. And in 1940, the German army went on the offensive and invaded France. It did not take very long after Paris fell. The next morning-- we were still in Orsay-- we heard the noise of marching outside. And we looked outside, very early in the morning, 5 o'clock, and they were French soldiers retreating. And then a French soldier came to our house and said take cover, because we're going to blow up the bridge. The highway they were on ran over a little creek, and there was this bridge. And about five minutes later, there was this huge explosion, and all the windows broken. And this was very close to the house we lived in. And the French had blown up that little bridge, to slow down the Germans. Well, it didn't slow down the Germans, because they simply went down a side street and bypassed the bridge and continued on the same highway. And the two ladies said, well, you know the Germans are here. Now we are the enemy, we're Polish. We can't keep this up. You have to leave. And we said, well, we don't know where to go. And they made a couple of phone calls. And they said that there is a French orphanage run by the French government, in a town called Sevres, which is near Versailles. And that's about 20 kilometers from here, about 14 miles. And you can take the bicycles. We had about four or five bicycles in the house that we would run errands on. And we just each took our clothes and some things that we could put in a backpack. And they're expecting you when you go there. But be careful, because you have to cross the German line.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we marched with some French soldiers who went up to meet the Germans on this road to Sevres. And after a while, the French soldiers turned back. But we had to go on. We saw some Germans, but they didn't bother us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to this place, which was a very large state orphanage. And there were about 500 children there. They were not Jewish children per se, we were the only Jewish children's group, about 8 or 10 of us that went there. [BACKGROUND NOISE] These were all French children. It was run by French people. And it was sort of, in retrospect, an Orphan Annie type place. You know, it was run by a couple of ladies who were a little mean and strict and-- at least to us children, they were. But they had a big responsibility, all these kids, and supply problems were huge and what have you. We stayed there until the Germans came in one day and controlled to find out who was there. And they asked these ladies if there were any Jewish children there. Of course, she said yes. And she pointed us out to them. And this German soldier came over to me. And he spoke to me in German. I had no choice, but I answered him in German.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said to me, if you do me a favor, I will do you a big favor. And I said, anything I can do. He says, I want to buy a motorcycle. And I understand there's a dealer in town, but I can't speak French. Can you help me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, OK I didn't ask him what his favor was, but I went with him to this motorcycle dealer. Because apparently by this time in Germany, you could not buy a motorcycle anymore. They were just making armaments.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I negotiated-- I was 14 years old, but I negotiated a deal for this motorcycle for this German soldier. And he bought it. And we walked out, he says now I will tell you something. But don't say to anyone where you heard this, or that I ever told you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, get away from this place where you are. That's all I can tell you. So I sort of knew what he meant. And I told the other Jewish kids. And we ran away at night. We, meaning my brother and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the soldier's name at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't know if I ever did. And I don't think he would have given it to me. I mean, I was-- what he did was very dangerous. First of all, he was not supposed to buy the motorcycle. I think that was legally. And secondly, that he would tell me that they had some sort orders to pick up these kids, that was absolutely, you know, against regulations.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you at this orphanage then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were at this residence I would say about three months, in this orphanage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was-- were there any groups that were helping you out with--

speaker: interviewee
text: At this point, no. At this point, we were wards of the French state. And the French state quasi did not exist anymore. Because it was occupied by the Germans. It was occupied France now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you and your brother go?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only place that we could think of where to go was to my aunt in Paris. And we weren't even sure that she was still there, but she was. And we told her what had happened, and she took us in. Now the Germans had invaded France. But they didn't invade all of France. They sort of stopped in the middle. And France was partitioned into German-occupied France and what was later known as Vichy France. Because an old World War I general, by the name of Petain, had negotiated an armistice with the Germans and gotten a promise that the southern third of France would not be occupied by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your aunt's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt's name was Greta, Greta Sinason. She was Richard's wife. And as I told you before, she was not born Jewish and had therefore kept her German passport. Obviously, she had barely enough to feed herself and her daughter. Her daughter, being half Jewish, was in hiding with some Catholic people. And she could not keep us. She simply could not financially afford to keep my brother and myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she tried to get in contact with one of those Jewish social organizations that had taken care of us before. But they had all fled into that unoccupied part of France, into Vichy France. And she called someone there. And they said, well, if you can get the boys to come here, we can take them into a home. But I don't know how you're going to do that, because the Germans are not letting any Jews cross the line. So my aunt said, OK, let me handle this. And the next morning she said to my brother and myself, without telling us what she was about to do, get dressed. Put on your best clothes and come with me. And we took the subway. And she went to gestapo headquarters in Paris with us. And I thought she was going to turn us over to the gestapo. And then she said in German to the receptionist, I want to see the person who's in charge of what's called [FRENCH], a pass for the frontier. And the receptionist said, are you German? She said, well, naturally I'm German. And she was blonde, blue-eyed, very German looking, very tall, statuesque type woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was very polite and clicked his heels. And he took her to the person that was in charge of this department. And she walked in there, speaking German. Of course, my brother and I understood. And she said, my name is Greta Sinason. I'm a German citizen. These two boys are my nephews. And she used our German names, Heinz and Gunther. And they are in boarding school in the south of France, because we want them to learn French. And now, all of a sudden I understand you need a special paper to cross the border. He said that's correct, Madame. Please give me the names again. He filled out two pieces of paper, handed it to her. And he said, please, sorry you had to inconvenience yourself and come here. And she marched out with us, and then she fainted in the street. Anyway. Excuse me. [DRINKS] She borrowed some money and bought us a train ticket. And she said, you go to Limoges, which was in unoccupied France. And here's the address. And you go to this place. And it's a children's home, and they will take care of you. It's run by the same people that took care of you before. [SIGHS] And We thanked her, and we got on the train, my brother and I. And we got to Limoges, and we asked somebody where this place was. And they said, well, you have to take a streetcar.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had a few pennies left, and we took the streetcar. And we arrived there. Again, it's always at 6 o'clock in the morning. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And these people said, oh, yes, you are the Sinason boys. We know you were coming. And they took us in. Now this home had changed hands. And there was a different social organization running it. And this organization played a very important part in our life, still does today. And this organization is called the OSE. It's simply an acronym. The letters O-S-E, with an accent on the e. In French, it's pronounced 'oh-zay.' It stands for Oeuvre de Secours aux Enfants, which means simply organization to help children. We had no idea who was funding them. But we knew, or we were told that they had a network of children's homes in unoccupied France. And that there were French Jewish children there, and also some German refugee and Austrian refugee children there in these homes. Now the home we landed in Limoges was really a home geared to small children-- babies, toddlers and kids of that age. And we were a little too big by this time, my brother and I. So after about three weeks there, they sent us to another home, which was about 100 miles or so north of Limoges but still in unoccupied France, in a department called Creuse. And the little-- tiny little village of about 100 people, was called Chabannes. And this big country house that this home was in now was called the Chateau de Chabannes. It wasn't really a chateau. It was just a nice old country house, sort of Georgian style.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to stop right here and go on to the next tape. Thanks.
